retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Urine Output and Interpretation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary or urine output (UO) is an important clinical indicator of renal physiology and function. Evaluating UO can provide insights into a patient's hydration status and guide subsequent management. A normal UO is approximately 0.5 to 1.0 mL/kg/h. Therefore, an average, healthy male with a body weight of 70 kg should produce around 35 to 70 mL of urine per hour.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Urine Output and Interpretation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In certain disease states affecting the kidney, a disease process called acute kidney injury (AKI) may arise, which can decrease the UO. The Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) criteria for defining an AKI encompasses UO as one of the markers of severity of AKI. According to these criteria, AKI is classified as stage 1 if the UO is less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 to 12 hours, stage 2 if the UO is less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for 12 hours or more, and stage 3 if the UO is less than 0.3 mL/kg/h for at least 24 hours or if there is anuria for at least 12 hours. [1] In other disease states affecting the kidney, UO may increase, resulting in a condition called polyuria. Polyuria in adults is defined as a UO exceeding 3 L per 24 hours or 50 mL/kg per 24 hours. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Failure Drug Dose Adjustments -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: CKD is defined as a glomerular filtration rate (GFR) lower than 60 mL/min or albumin higher than 30 mg/g of creatinine, accompanied by abnormalities of kidney structure or function for more than 3 months. End-stage renal disease is a GFR of less than 15 mL/min.

retrieved_docs.title: Microalbuminuria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Through the dysfunction of the GBM filtration barrier, albumin can be secreted into the urine, and the amount that is present is important. [1] The current definition of microalbuminuria (MA) is an amount of urinary albumin greater than the normal value but also lower than what is detected by a conventional dipstick. Thus, the rate of urine albumin excretion (UAE) in microalbuminuria is 30 to 300 mg/24 hours. In other units, it can also mean 30–300 mcg/mg creatinine or 20–200 mcg/min on two out of three urine collections. This value is derived from studies that evaluated adults but could also be applied to the pediatric population. [2] [3] Macroalbuminuria, on the other hand, is classified as greater than 100 mg/12 hours or 300 mg/24 hours. The diagnosis of diabetic kidney disease requires a person with type 1 or 2 diabetes to have persistently elevated albuminuria (more than 300 mg/24 hours), diabetic retinopathy, and the absence of other kidney diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemodialysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The timing for initiation of dialysis is decided after considering the complications of early initiation (unnecessary exposure to IV lines and invasive procedure with risks of infection) against late initiation, causing avoidable volume, metabolic, and electrolyte complications of AKI. Assigning arbitrary urea nitrogen or creatinine level for dialysis initiation is not advisable due to individual variability in uremia symptom severity and renal function. Despite optimal CKD management, patients progress to needing RRT, especially when their eGFR drops below 20 ml/ min/1.73 m2 or they rapidly deteriorate to ESRD within 12 months. The eGFR at dialysis initiation has steadily increased in recent times. In 1996, in the United States, 13% of incident ESRD patients started RRT at an eGFR of 10 ml/min/1.73 m2 or higher. This increased to 43% in 2010 and dropped to 39% in 2015. Waiting for uremic symptoms to set in before commencing RRT had added risks of the patient being malnourished with increased mortality risk. Asking patients to compare their current eating habits and physical activity levels with those 6 to 12 months back helps avoid the lack of awareness. The concept of a 'healthy start,' with dialysis commencing before the onset of severe uremia symptoms, is associated with prolonged survival. An early start will prepone the need for a change of modality or further procedures without any improvement in the quality of life while adding to healthcare costs. The Renal Physicians Association's (RPA) criteria for identifying dialysis patients with a poor prognosis beyond 75 years of age includes:

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Clearance -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the concept of drug clearance is essential when determining the dosing of medications. When a medication is administered intravenously, the drug ends up either in the blood plasma or redistributes into the extravascular volume. The drug present in the plasma can be removed from the body primarily through the kidneys and liver. Drug clearance is defined as the volume of plasma cleared of a drug over a specified time period. [1] Thus, the unit of measurement for drug clearance is volume/time. Another equation can calculate clearance. Clearance is equal to the rate at which a drug is removed from plasma(mg/min) divided by the concentration of that drug in the plasma (mg/mL). The total ability of the body to clear a drug from the plasma is renal clearance plus hepatic clearance plus clearance from all other tissues. It is important to be aware of the fact that clearance does not tell us the amount of drug cleared. For example, let’s say drug X has a renal clearance of 20 mL/min and hepatic clearance of 5 mL/min. The total clearance of drug X would be 25 mL of plasma is cleared of drug X per minute.

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Urea cycle disorders are inborn errors of metabolism resulting from defects in one of the enzymes or transporter molecules involved in the hepatic removal of ammonia from the bloodstream. Removal of ammonia from the bloodstream normally occurs via its conversion to urea, which is then excreted by the kidneys. Consequently, urea cycle disorders lead to an accumulation of ammonia. Ammonia is extremely toxic, particularly to the central nervous system. Newborns with severe mutations in any one of the first four enzymes of the urea cycle can become catastrophically ill within 36 to 48 hours of birth despite appearing normal at birth. It is, therefore, possible for a newborn to be discharged from the hospital before signs of urea cycle disorders develop. The newborn may subsequently develop signs that go unrecognized at home. Hyperammonemia is key to the diagnosis of urea cycle disorders, and treatment should not be delayed while a definitive diagnosis is sought. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of urea cycle disorders and highlights the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance outcomes for affected patients and families.

retrieved_docs.title: Elimination Half-Life of Drugs -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Equation 1: ln[ A 0 ]/[ A ] =kt Equation 2: t 1/2 =0.693/ k Equation 3: t 1/2 =0.693 ×V d /CL, where V d is the volume of distribution and CL is clearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Failure Drug Dose Adjustments -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Renal impairment modifies the effects of many medications, occasionally diminishing them but more typically enhancing or even multiplying them, thus leading to accumulation and potential toxicity. Many of these alterations can be and should be predicted and subsequently alleviated by adjusting drug doses. In summary, the medication dose should be decreased equivalently to the calculated reduction of drug clearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Failure Drug Dose Adjustments -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The effects of many drugs are altered in renal impairment, particularly when a drug is renally cleared. Drug doses should be altered based on the predicted reduction in the clearance of the drug. Patient factors, such as the degree of renal disease and patient size, also influence the decision to adjust drug doses. In addition, drug factors, including the drug excretion and the therapeutic index, must be considered when adjusting doses. Accurate estimation of renal function is crucial for determining appropriate doses of renally excreted drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Nephelometry is a technique to measure the turbidity of a solution, which helps diagnose and monitor conditions like liver disease or kidney failure. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury (AKI), formerly known as acute renal failure (ARF), denotes a sudden and often reversible reduction in kidney function, as measured by glomerular filtration rate (GFR). [1] [2] [3] There is no clear definition of AKI. Several different criteria have been used in research studies, such as RIFLE, AKIN (Acute Kidney Injury Network), or KDIGO (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes) criteria. However, KDIGO is the most recent and most commonly used. According to KDIGO, AKI is the presence of any of the following: Increase in serum creatinine by 0.3 mg/dL or more (26.5 micromoles/L or more) within 48 hours Increase in serum creatinine to 1.5 times or more baseline, within the prior 7 days Urine volume less than  0.5 mL/kg/h for at least 6 hours

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Failure Drug Dose Adjustments -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a type of kidney disorder characterized by a gradual decline in kidney function that occurs over several months to years. [1] Although various organs contribute to drug metabolism and excretion, the liver is the primary site for metabolism, and the kidneys are primarily responsible for excretion.

retrieved_docs.title: Ranson Criteria -- Function -- Ranson Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Serum calcium less than 8.0 mg/dL (<2.0 mmol/L) Hematocrit falls greater than 10% PaO2 less than 60 mmHg BUN increased by 5 mg/dL or more (≥1.8 mmol/L ) despite intravenous (IV) fluid hydration Base deficit greater than 4 mEq/L Sequestration of fluids greater than 6 L

retrieved_docs.title: Prerenal Kidney Failure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: KDIGO defines AKI by the use of three major criteria, which are; an increase in creatinine (Cr) higher than 0.3 mg/dl (26.5 umol/L) in 48 hours, a rise of creatine greater than 1.5 times the baseline (presumed to have occurred in the last seven days), and a decrease of urine volume of equal to or lesser than 0.5 ml/kg/hr. If one or more of these criteria are met, the patient can be diagnosed with an AKI. The third criterion (decline in urine output) is a topic of debate, as many healthy individuals would meet this criterion if they had limited fluid intake throughout the day. Therefore caution is advised in making the diagnosis solely off of the last criterion alone. It is important to remember that the authors of KDIGO criteria stress that although these guidelines provide a framework to diagnose AKI, they should not replace clinical gestalt. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2795
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Nephrology Terminology includes the concept of قصور الكلى (renal failure). Within this clinical framework, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى, indicating that the supplement should be avoided in individuals who have renal failure. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians referencing Pediatric Nephrology Terminology to ensure safe medication management for patients with kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrate (fenofibrát) is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals whose kidney function is impaired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى (renal failure), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals suffering from kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2621
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerulopathy) is a disorder affecting the glomeruli of the kidneys, and it can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), a condition characterized by the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products in the blood due to impaired renal filtration. This connection highlights that damage to the kidney’s filtering units may lead to systemic toxicity, emphasizing the need for early identification and treatment of glomerular disease to prevent the development of uremic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: CPTAC Codelists Terminology and CPTAC Terminology both incorporate the concept of غسيل الكلى غير محدد (unspecified dialysis) within their respective subsets, indicating that this dialysis category is recognized and included in each of these CPTAC terminology collections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 412
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder), indicating that its use should be avoided in individuals with impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1596
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure, which is classified as فشل كلوي حاد, is clinically associated with الحماض (acidosis) and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia). The presence of الحماض and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم frequently co‑occurs, reflecting their linked pathophysiology in renal impairment. Moreover, acute and unspecified renal failure is considered related to, and possibly synonymous with, وكلاء الكليون السفلي (lower urinary tract agents), indicating an overlap in terminology or clinical context between these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2704
retrieved_docs.content: CPTAC Codelists Terminology and CPTAC Terminology each include the concepts غسيل الكلى غير محدد (unspecified dialysis) and جراحة تقليل حجم الرئة (lung volume reduction surgery), indicating that both terminological collections recognize these medical procedures as part of their respective subsets.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac function is a broad physiological concept that includes measures such as معدل ضربات القلب (heart rate). In clinical practice, the evaluation of cardiac function is interpreted specifically in the context of congestive heart failure in patients who are non‑hypertensive, providing important information for diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: The term “komplikace” (complication) is described as being able to be qualified by specific conditions, and the descriptors “Allowed qualifier” and “can be qualified by.” indicate that certain entities are permissible qualifiers for this category. In particular, the complication category can be qualified by diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), by other complications of pregnancy, and by death (التموت). This framing underscores that diabetic kidney disease, pregnancy‑related complications, and mortality are recognized as valid examples of medical complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1988
retrieved_docs.content: CPTAC Codelists Terminology encompasses the concept of غسيل الكلى غير محدد, indicating that this terminology set includes an entry for unspecified dialysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1891
retrieved_docs.content: Aktivní uhlí (activated charcoal) is a medical adsorbent commonly employed for gastrointestinal decontamination after ingestion of toxins, while اليوريمية (uremia) is a metabolic complication of severe renal failure that typically requires renal replacement therapies such as dialysis. Activated charcoal is not an appropriate or effective treatment for uremia, and the two concepts are not directly therapeutic to one another; instead, uremia is managed with modalities that restore kidney function rather than with gastrointestinal adsorbents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1117
retrieved_docs.content: to the clinical syndrome of rapid loss of renal function. Finally, the value “Unknown” is listed as a permissible entry for variables related to chronic kidney disease, reflecting the need to capture cases where the specific renal diagnosis cannot be determined. Together, these relationships illustrate how anemia, hypertension, various forms of glomerulonephritis, and acute renal failure are interconnected within the taxonomy of kidney disease, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and research classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is recognized as a general clinical state that clinicians interpret, and it is associated with the condition described in Arabic as الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, which denotes combined hepatic and renal failure. Understanding this relationship aids in the assessment of patients with multi‑organ dysfunction and informs appropriate management strategies.
